sentences.text: God almighty, please give me a break.
Well, as I said, we started to go to apply for a different place to go.
The only thing that they took-- we were numbers.
We were not names.
So we went in.
Again, we had to undress, walk in front of the assessment, and whoever was accepted they took the number of what we had on the dress-- well, dress.
You should pardon the expression.
Anyhow, four of us went through it.
One wasn't accepted.
She was too thin.
I took her sweater.
I went in again and passed her number.
(WHISPERING) Oh, god I don't know.
I don't remember.
Well if she would have-- if she would have survived, I would have remembered.
We went to Skarzyska quickly.
It was in an ammunition factory.
For that, we had to parade naked, right?
To go to a-- to go to it.
And all I had is a half-torn dress, that I wound up there.
One time, I went and-- I'll skip it maybe.
Because I--
Hmm?
Well I went over one Sunday when we were not going to work.
I went over to a-- to the commandant, which you know already what commandant means.
And he was like a hunchback, and he was a regular killer.
He had a dog that he used to talk to the dog to-- to tear apart people.
Tinneman.
Kinnaman-- or whatever, something like that.
I went over.
And he was with the Jewish commandant there, and I wanted to talk to him.
Yeah, they had to have a Jewish person also to run with the people.
Well, I went over to him, and he said go away.
He was afraid that the dog is going to tear me apart.
And I said, please listen to me-- by then I had learned a little German.
And I-- and I-- and I turned around and I showed him how my dress is in shreds, and I have to go in night-- night-- the shift to go to work at night.
And-- and it's cold, and I'm freezing.
And he took me in.
Meanwhile, everybody in the-- in the barracks thought that I am not coming back.
He talk-- he took me in.
My parents were praying there is some place in heaven for me.
And he took me in into the-- the-- there was a shoe store-- not store.
They were working with shoes, and they were working with different things for--
What?
They were doing all kinds of tailoring things.
He took me in.
And he said you have something that was made something out of a heavy bathrobe-- the nice thick bathrobes that they used.
And it wasn't ready, and he said, tomorrow you come in, and you get it.
I'll tell you something-- somebody up there loved me because I don't know.
I came in, and they saw me alive.
And I said wait until you see me tomorrow.
Because of that I have a kidney beaten down.
Because when they took us out from Skarzyska to Schonster Haus, and I worked night shift.
And my three machines-- you know, they break down.
So quickly, they had to-- they-- I didn't make the-- during the night, when the-- the-- the big shot from the-- you know, the commandant, came by, and I didn't have the amount.
So they took several people out for the [NON-ENGLISH]-- to the place where they had the office, and they gave us a beating.
And being that I don't know if I was-- didn't want to give in, or it was that nice thick skirt that I was wearing-- he picked up the skirt, and they were beating me on the kidneys.
And I felt like something snapped.
For weeks, I was sleeping on my tummy, because it was as black as-- as my slacks, yeah.
So that was one thing.
Oh yeah, I had to go after the beating, I had to go back to the machines.
A bullet-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] those things for the bullets.
The cases for bullets.
So anyhow, by the time I want to get into-- that the Russian home front was nearing-- so they-- that date I remember.
January 16, 1945.
They took us all, lead us all into the-- into the-- wagons, and took us to Ravensbruck.
Now, Ravensbruck-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Majdanek was bad enough.
It probably looked like it was clean and all, but Ravensbruck was just-- people were dropping like flies.
And we were-- we had to do like leveling a hill.
We had to do like they made a whole line of women, and each had a shovel.
And the one from the top was giving it to the next one, and the next one.
That's how we leveled a hill.
Yes, we were together all the time, thank God.
And-- and they made us carry boulders, like I said, from one place to the other.
The-- the smell of the-- of the-- of the ovens was devastating.
And every-- every day there were selections that they took into the gas chambers.
I don't know.
I told you, I had-- I had-- I had scurvy.
You know what scurvy is?
From lack of certain foods-- being that I'm so delicate.
I'm sorry.
And I developed all those-- all those sores.
And they used to get in the-- when we were working outside, then mud used to get into them.
But what I said, they didn't even know that they were taking care of me.
Because we were getting food with mold.
Makes sense?
Mold makes penicillin.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From there, they took us to two smaller camps-- Burgau and Turkheim.
And then we started to hear all those-- all those planes.
And we were praying to God they should throw some bombs down, because we-- we couldn't take it anymore.
We were like skeletons.
All of a sudden, one day, they marched us out from the-- from the barracks, and they didn't have any wagons.
They didn't have any-- any trucks, and they marched us.
Now, they were marching us for five days.
We didn't know where we were going.
People were dropping like flies.
We were walking-- yes.
We were walking-- yes-- we were walking.
Whoever remained, that they couldn't go, they were shooting.
But they used to keep us at night, in bombed-out ditches like, full of water from the rains, and that's how we slept-- in the water.
During the day, we were-- when we-- when we stopped in one field-- now everything is being washed and clean and everything.
And one time we were left-- we were-- I mean, they left us and-- and relax a little bit in a field.
And there were manure ready for spreading.
There were half-rotten carrots in it.
I wiped it in warm water to wash, wiped it on my dress, and that's what I ate.
And that's all 2 true.
Now, f everything I wash-- everything has to be scrubbed.
At the time, nothing happened to me.
Well, after the five days, we were supposed to arrive in the afternoon to Dachau.
We say Dachau here-- in English, you say Dachau.
No-- we-- we were supposed to be exterminated when we came in.
But we were delayed.
So we arrived in the middle of the night, during the night.
Because the-- the soldiers, themselves, couldn't walk that much anymore.

And one time, they got in touch with somebody that they came-- delivered some kind of little foods to-- for the people to be able to march, and get them into-- to the place where they were going to exterminate us.
And-- and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the morning, we-- we barely saw any SS.
We didn't know what happened.
Then we found out they started to run away.
Coming in during the-- late at night saved us.
Because-- oh, when we arrived, the SS women-- let me tell you something.
If you see-- if you ever heard about SS men being the most horrible killers, the women-- SS women-- were worse.
I encountered them in Majdanek, I encountered them in Ravensbruck.
Everything you want-- thick.
And-- and the--
No, most of them ran away, and then they made us come-- go into the barracks where the Nazis soldiers used to occupy.
And I still didn't believe that we are being-- I said we're going to get it for taking over those things.
Oh, the men that came from the men's side of the camp.
No, and then-- and then I-- we started to tear the pictures of Goering and Hitler off the walls.
And I said, oh, we are going to get it for doing that.
And during the night, the Hitlerjugend-- the Hitler Youth-- were shooting at the-- at the camps.
And one doctor that went out--
From the hiding places-- you'd be surprised how they were brainwashing those-- those young kids.
They were always around.
No, but outside the camp, they were shooting up.
And they were--
No people killed.
To the girl that I saved in Ravensbruck, she-- she developed an illness and she died when they were taking us into the wagons.
She died in the wagon.
That's what I want to clear something-- the other two, we lost touch because-- But when the American troops walked in-- this is something, I am holding back tears.
Because I don't know where the men got hold of all kinds of-- of flags from the whole world, flags.
The men got hold of flags that the Germans got-- you know, put away.
No, they were not Jewish men.
They were-- there were all kinds.
I was with nuns in the concentration camps.
There were-- there were priests in the concentration camps.
But they got hold of flags.
And the gate to come in-- there was-- it was so wide, that you could, several trucks, going in and out at the same time.
They opened all the-- the gates, and we started-- some on all four, because they let us know that the soldiers are coming-- and we went out to greet the American soldiers.
And we dropped to their feet.
And we kissed their boots.
There were some of them that couldn't walk.
They were just crawling.
They picked up and carried the men into the camps.
And if you-- and if-- and if Hollywood would put something like that, they would say oh, God, another Hollywood thing.
But this is something that stays so much in me.
First of all, the first thing that they did is spray us with DDT-- which is outlawed now-- because we were with lice-- head lice, clothes lice.
We were-- they were eating us up alive.
Every time we killed a-- excuse me-- a louse, it was full of blood.
Then they started to give us food, and a lot of people died.
Because their-- they couldn't-- their system couldn't take it anymore.
And we were watching-- we were like five-- and we're watching one another not to overeat-- we would watch one another to eat within limits, because a lot of people died.
I had a woman that walked next to me-- well, I was a young kid then.
She was a middle-aged woman, and she said, oh, God, I would like to live once more to eat a decent meal.
I don't care if I die.
She had her wish.
I saw her die after she ate.
They didn't realize in the beginning, right in the beginning, not to give us all that food.
No.
No, my sister was more optimistic.
I was the biggest pessimist.
I don't know.
My sister was pushing me.
And what made me feel alive?
I think God wanted me to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somebody that goes to-- to talk to kids and talk about-- about the Holocaust-- an eyewitness.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Had a hard life-- I came to the United States without a pot to spit in.
In Berlin.
I came to Berlin.
I met my husband.
Leo.
And we got-- we were liberated in 1945, the end of April.
And I met-- then I met my husband.
I tried to go-- I tried to get in touch with family in the United States, but we didn't know their right number.
I stopped going-- because I found out that-- atrocities, again.
They were killing people again.
They were having-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- my sister-in-law said, so many of you yet remained alive-- the American-- when they were making pogroms.
A friend of ours-- his brother was killed in the pogroms.
So I didn't-- I had no heart to go back.
So in Berlin, to begin with, noting because we were getting better ration cards.
And then I started to go-- my sister and I lived in a rented room.
And we were going to synagogue-- in the same building was a synagogue.
In Berlin.
In [NON-ENGLISH].
So we met-- we met a-- a captain, a Jewish captain.
And-- and we were telling him that we wrote a letter to the United States, and-- and it came back.
I have the letter.
I should have had it here.
Anyhow, he said, I'll take care of it.
He wrote to the Jewish newspaper, forward to New York, that two sisters are from wherever, then the first names, and all, are looking for a brother Leo Fromer, and an aunt Rhea Levingrup.
My Aunt Rhea was sitting having breakfast, and I knocked on the door.
Her sister-in-law comes in.
She says, look, is that your family?
Rhea Levingrup lived in-- in New York-- in Brooklyn, excuse me.
And she-- my brother lived in-- in-- in New York, in Manhattan.
And she said is that you?
She started to cry, my sister's children are alive.
And they got in touch with us.

Then, I found out that my oldest brother, that ran away with his wife and child to Russia-- because, you know, they were killing people that were-- my brother was running the first aid station also during the bombarding.
So they-- they were alive, and they came back.
And they got in touch with my brother, here in the United States.
And that's when I found out that they are-- they are alive.
And then they started to send us packages with food.
So between the-- between the better ration card, and the packages, we were not starving.
And we were-- we-- so I met my husband, and we got married in 1946.
In Berlin.
No.
At that time by then, we rented an apartment to stay.
And-- and-- and we did the wedding right there.
We invited some people, and some people that were, you know, German-Jewish people that came back.
And they helped us out with putting together little foods and things.
And then they opened up a kosher butcher stores, and we could buy it on the ration cards, also.
So they chipped in with ration cards for the-- and-- and so we got married.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yes.
No.
And I-- and a very religious German-Jewish people were the witnesses and things.
We lost track.
The-- and-- the-- so we got married.
But then, my brother sent, you know, to bring us to the United States.
And I became pregnant right away-- nine and a half months later, my son was born.
But when I became pregnant, they said for me to wait until I gave birth to my son.
They want to see what kind of child I give birth to-- if it's a normal child.
I don't know.
I don't know.
No.
It was a Jewish doctor from the Iranian-Jewish hospital.
He took care of me-- I can't begin to tell you.
Because you know, any people that came out from the camps-- first of all, when I stood up and put my legs together, I didn't think that they're supposed to-- flesh was supposed to meet flesh.
Eventually it did.
That's how undernourished we were when we were liberated.
I'm going to tell you one more thing about the racism of Polish people.
One time we were--
Yes, anti-Semitism.
And one time my sister and I, we are standing at one of-- coming from Munich towards Berlin.
We were waiting-- maybe we will get on a train.
And there were some people sitting around waiting, also.
And we got to talking.
And they were talking Polish.
And education in-- for Jewish kids in Warsaw was more like the refined English you hear like from England or South Africa.
Do you know that my sister-in-law talked to a man, a Polish man, during the time when they started to send people to the camps and into the ovens.
And he said, God, that's a-- that is the most delicious smell-- the Jewish fat frying.
I didn't hear it from a strange person-- my own sister-in-law.
He didn't even know she was Jewish at the time when he told her that.
That's the kind of-- I'm not saying that all Polish people are bad.
We had people that used to come to our home where they were friends that they would kill for us.
And they participated in dinners, and they used to keep their heads or caps on because of respect for my father-- because he was wearing a cover on his head during the meal time.
So we-- so we finally came from-- from-- excuse me-- from Bremen to the United States.
On a ship that was-- oh, how that ship didn't drown, I'll never know.
I was so sick-- Marlin, Marlene, or something like that.
And it's surprising it didn't drown.
And I was so sick all-- the whole time.
And my poor child-- he was in diapers, you know, it was burnt and all.
And-- and my brother picked us up.
April the 10th 1948.
Just before Pesach.
At my brother's house.
Strange.
Honey, for years, you-- we didn't know about the holiday.
We didn't know about the Shabbos.
We didn't know about anything.
The first time I started with [NON-ENGLISH], that I didn't have all the pots and pans.
But when-- when I was in Berlin, and when we had started to have a kosher butcher, that was the first time I tasted a piece of kosher meat.
I remember, during a Seder, when my brothers used to-- when you read.
And they would used to go trying to mumble a little bit.
And my father used to go like, ahem, ahem.
Because he wanted to hear them out loud.
[LAUGHS] So you see, certain things you start remembering during the holiday.
And those are the worst times.
They were happy times.
In New York?
First of all, I went into-- I didn't want to stay with my brother because, you know, some wives cannot be-- not the second.
His second wife was marvelous.
I'm still friends with her, even though my brother is dead.
She lives in Las Vegas.
She's-- she's a sister, not a sister-in-law.
So I went into a place where it was-- a cold flat, with a toilet outside, and the bath tub in the kitchen.
And my husband started to work, and he was making-- and we always say it.
You know, it's an expression in Russian-- [SPEAKING RUSSIAN], meaning half of nothing.
That-- and I had an infant.
And being that I was brought up with a live-in maid.
And I said to my Aunt Rhea, please, find me a place where I can go and scrub somebody's toilets.
And I went to the butcher, bought a small piece of meat, used extra potatoes that it would fill up the stomach.
But I always tried to feed the stomach with the right kind of food.
Yes, I was pregnant three more times.
Twice I had miscarriages.
And one time, being that I worked very hard work on a farm, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was hemorrhaging for six weeks until, she was taken by-- by C-section.
And she died after two days.
This is the end.
